description: sp, sp², sp³ hybridization, sigma and pi bonds, intermolecular forces (London, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding), and physical property predictions.

problems.problem: Define hybridization. Why do atoms undergo hybridization?

step_by_step.explanation: Hybridization is the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals of equal energy.
Atoms undergo hybridization to maximize orbital overlap and achieve more stable bonding arrangements that match observed molecular geometries.

problems.final_answer: Hybridization is the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals; it allows atoms to form stable bonds with correct geometry

problems.problem: What is sp hybridization? What molecular geometry does it produce? Give an example.

step_by_step.explanation: One s orbital and one p orbital mix to form two equivalent sp hybrid orbitals.
sp hybridization produces linear geometry (180° bond angle).

problems.final_answer: sp hybridization → linear geometry (180°); example: CO₂

problems.problem: What is sp² hybridization? What molecular geometry does it produce? Give an example.

step_by_step.explanation: One s orbital and two p orbitals mix to form three equivalent sp² hybrid orbitals.
sp² hybridization produces trigonal planar geometry (120° bond angle).

problems.final_answer: sp² hybridization → trigonal planar geometry (120°); example: BF₃

problems.problem: What is sp³ hybridization? What molecular geometry does it produce? Give an example.

step_by_step.explanation: One s orbital and three p orbitals mix to form four equivalent sp³ hybrid orbitals.
sp³ hybridization produces tetrahedral geometry (109.5° bond angle).

problems.final_answer: sp³ hybridization → tetrahedral geometry (109.5°); example: CH₄

problems.problem: Describe the difference between sigma (σ) bonds and pi (π) bonds.

step_by_step.explanation: Sigma bonds form from end-to-end overlap of orbitals; electron density is concentrated along the internuclear axis.
Pi bonds form from side-to-side overlap of p orbitals; electron density is above and below the internuclear axis.
Sigma bonds are stronger than pi bonds because of greater orbital overlap.

problems.final_answer: σ bonds: end-to-end overlap, stronger; π bonds: side-to-side overlap, weaker

problems.problem: How many sigma and pi bonds are present in a molecule of ethene (C₂H₄)?

step_by_step.explanation: Each C is sp² hybridized; double bond between C atoms.

step_by_step.calculation: C-H bonds (4) are sigma; C-C single bond is sigma → total 5 sigma bonds
The second bond in the C=C double bond is a pi bond → 1 pi bond

problems.final_answer: 5 sigma bonds, 1 pi bond

problems.problem: How many sigma and pi bonds are present in a molecule of ethyne (C₂H₂)?

step_by_step.explanation: Each C is sp hybridized; triple bond between C atoms.

step_by_step.calculation: C-H bonds (2) are sigma; one bond in the triple bond is sigma → total 3 sigma bonds
The remaining two bonds in the triple bond are pi bonds → 2 pi bonds

problems.final_answer: 3 sigma bonds, 2 pi bonds

problems.problem: What hybridization does an atom with 5 electron domains (bonding pairs + lone pairs) exhibit?

step_by_step.explanation: 5 domains → sp³d hybridization

step_by_step.calculation: P in PCl₅, S in SF₄, Cl in ClF₃

problems.final_answer: sp³d hybridization (trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry)

problems.problem: What hybridization does an atom with 6 electron domains (bonding pairs + lone pairs) exhibit?

step_by_step.explanation: 6 domains → sp³d² hybridization

step_by_step.calculation: S in SF₆, Xe in XeF₄

problems.final_answer: sp³d² hybridization (octahedral electron domain geometry)

problems.problem: Determine the hybridization of the central atom in methane (CH₄).

step_by_step.explanation: C has 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs
4 domains → sp³ hybridization

problems.final_answer: sp³

problems.problem: Determine the hybridization of the central atom in boron trifluoride (BF₃).

step_by_step.explanation: B has 3 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs
3 domains → sp² hybridization

problems.final_answer: sp²

problems.problem: Determine the hybridization of the central atom in beryllium chloride (BeCl₂).

step_by_step.explanation: Be has 2 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs
2 domains → sp hybridization

problems.final_answer: sp

problems.problem: Determine the hybridization of the central atom in water (H₂O).

step_by_step.explanation: O has 2 bonding pairs, 2 lone pairs
4 domains → sp³ hybridization

problems.final_answer: sp³

problems.problem: Determine the hybridization of the central atom in ammonia (NH₃).

step_by_step.explanation: N has 3 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair
4 domains → sp³ hybridization

problems.final_answer: sp³

problems.problem: Determine the hybridization of the central atom in sulfur hexafluoride (SF₆).

step_by_step.explanation: S has 6 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs (expanded octet)
6 domains → sp³d² hybridization

problems.final_answer: sp³d²

problems.problem: Determine the hybridization of the central atom in phosphorus pentachloride (PCl₅).

step_by_step.explanation: P has 5 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs (expanded octet)
5 domains → sp³d hybridization

problems.final_answer: sp³d

problems.problem: List the three main types of intermolecular forces in order of increasing strength.

step_by_step.explanation: London dispersion forces (temporary dipoles)
Dipole-dipole forces (permanent dipoles)
Hydrogen bonding (special dipole-dipole with H bonded to N, O, or F)

problems.final_answer: London dispersion < dipole-dipole < hydrogen bonding

problems.problem: What factors affect the strength of London dispersion forces?

step_by_step.explanation: Molar mass (larger mass = more electrons = stronger dispersion forces)
Molecular shape (longer, more elongated molecules have stronger dispersion forces than compact molecules)
Surface area (larger surface area = stronger dispersion forces)

problems.final_answer: Molar mass, molecular shape, and surface area

problems.problem: Explain why water has a higher boiling point than methane (CH₄).

step_by_step.explanation: Water exhibits hydrogen bonding (strong IMF)
Methane only exhibits London dispersion forces (weak IMF)
Stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome, resulting in higher boiling point

problems.final_answer: Water has hydrogen bonding (strong); methane has only dispersion forces (weak); stronger IMF = higher boiling point

problems.problem: Explain why hydrogen fluoride (HF) has a higher boiling point than hydrogen chloride (HCl).

step_by_step.explanation: HF exhibits hydrogen bonding (H bonded to F)
HCl has dipole-dipole forces but not hydrogen bonding (H bonded to Cl, but Cl is less electronegative)
Hydrogen bonding is stronger than dipole-dipole forces

problems.final_answer: HF has hydrogen bonding; HCl has only dipole-dipole forces; hydrogen bonding is stronger, so HF has higher boiling point

problems.problem: Predict which has a higher boiling point: propane (C₃H₈) or butane (C₄H₁₀). Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Both are non-polar molecules; only London dispersion forces are present

step_by_step.calculation: Butane (C₄H₁₀) has higher molar mass than propane (C₃H₈)

step_by_step.explanation: Higher molar mass = more electrons = stronger London dispersion forces = higher boiling point

problems.final_answer: Butane has higher boiling point due to higher molar mass and stronger London dispersion forces

problems.problem: Explain the principle of "like dissolves like."

step_by_step.explanation: Polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents; non-polar solutes dissolve in non-polar solvents
Molecules with similar intermolecular forces interact favorably; energy is released when similar IMF are formed

step_by_step.calculation: Water (polar) dissolves salt (polar/ionic); oil (non-polar) dissolves grease (non-polar)

problems.final_answer: Like dissolves like: polar dissolves polar, non-polar dissolves non-polar; similar IMF promote solubility

problems.problem: Predict whether hexane (C₆H₁₄) will dissolve in water. Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Hexane is a non-polar hydrocarbon (only C-C and C-H bonds, symmetric)
Water is a polar molecule with hydrogen bonding
Non-polar solutes do not dissolve well in polar solvents; water and hexane have very different IMF

problems.final_answer: No, hexane is non-polar and water is polar; non-polar substances do not dissolve well in polar solvents

problems.problem: Arrange the following substances in order of increasing boiling point: CH₄, H₂O, CH₃Cl, C₄H₁₀.

step_by_step.explanation: Non-polar, only dispersion forces, low molar mass → lowest boiling point
Non-polar, only dispersion forces, higher molar mass than CH₄
Polar molecule with dipole-dipole forces (no hydrogen bonding)
Hydrogen bonding → highest boiling point

problems.final_answer: CH₄ < C₄H₁₀ < CH₃Cl < H₂O

problems.problem: Explain why ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH) is miscible with water, while ethane (CH₃CH₃) is not.

step_by_step.explanation: Ethanol has an -OH group capable of hydrogen bonding with water
Ethane is non-polar with only dispersion forces
Ethanol (polar, hydrogen bonding) dissolves in water (polar, hydrogen bonding); ethane (non-polar) does not

problems.final_answer: Ethanol can hydrogen bond with water; ethane cannot; polar molecules dissolve in polar solvents

problems.problem: A molecule has the formula C₂H₄. Identify its hybridization, bond angles, and the number of sigma and pi bonds.

step_by_step.explanation: Each C has 3 electron domains (2 single bonds to H, 1 double bond to other C)
3 domains → sp² hybridization

step_by_step.calculation: C-H bonds (4) + C-C sigma bond (1) = 5 sigma bonds
The second bond in the C=C double bond is a pi bond = 1 pi bond

problems.final_answer: sp² hybridization; bond angles ≈120°; 5 sigma bonds, 1 pi bond
